title: Chapter 4 Quiz
description: Test your stamina in loops, iterations, and control flow in Go!

questions.question: How many main types of loops does Go have?
questions.explanation: Go simplifies iteration safely down to ONLY ONE keyword: `for`.

questions.question: You use `while` in Go to create loops that run purely based on a condition.
questions.explanation: Go doesn't have a `while` keyword. You just use `for` with only the condition part!

questions.question: What is the correct syntax for an infinite loop?
questions.explanation: A bare `for {}` is the cleanest, most idiomatic way to write an infinite loop in Go.

questions.question: Which keyword is used to instantly exit a loop entirely?
questions.explanation: `break` terminates the loop completely.

questions.question: Which keyword skips the rest of the current loop iteration and starts the next one right away? _____
questions.explanation: The `continue` keyword allows skipping early without stopping the entire loop.

questions.question: What are the three components of a classic `for` loop, in order?
questions.explanation: A standard loop sets up a variable (`init`), checks it (`condition`), and modifies it at the end of the block (`post`).

questions.question: In the statement `for i := 0; i < 5; i++`, what does the `i++` part do?
options: Increases i by 1 after the code block runs
questions.explanation: The post statement `i++` increments the counter, bringing it closer to the condition limit.

questions.question: It's illegal to leave out the init and post statements in a `for` loop.
questions.explanation: Go actually encourages this! By leaving out `init` and `post`, you create exactly what other languages call a `while` loop.

questions.question: If you create a `while`-style loop in Go (only using the condition), do you use semicolons?
options: Yes, you must have two empty semicolons: `for ; x > 0 ;`
No, you drop semicolons entirely: `for x > 0`
questions.explanation: The syntax is incredibly clean: `for condition {` with absolutely no semicolons.

questions.question: What does the powerful keyword `range` do?
options: Validates if a number is within a specific range
Iterates over items in collections like strings or arrays
questions.explanation: `range` unpacks collections, giving you access to each underlying element automatically.

questions.question: When iterating with `range`, it returns two pieces of data. What are they?
questions.explanation: `range` always hands you the index (the position 0, 1, 2) and the value at that position.

questions.question: In Go, if you declare a variable inside a loop using `:=`, it will cause the program to crash if you don't use it.
questions.explanation: Go strictly enforces that all variables legally declared must be utilized, preventing wasted memory.

questions.question: To ignore the index returned by `range` and only capture the value, we use the `_____` symbol (the blank identifier).
questions.explanation: The underscore `_` essentially tells Go to discard the returned value safely.

questions.question: Which one of these does Go lack completely?
questions.explanation: Go has no `do-while`, `while`, or `until` constructs; every looping construct is based solely on `for`.

questions.question: What is the output of this code? `for i := 0; i < 3; i++ { if i==1 { continue } fmt.Print(i) }`
questions.explanation: When `i==1`, `continue` skips the print statement, moving to `i==2`. So it outputs `02`.

questions.question: Which of these is the correct Go syntax?
questions.explanation: Again, Go actively omits the use of parentheses around control flow logic requirements.

questions.question: A loop condition is evaluated at which point in the cycle?
questions.explanation: The condition checks before the logic triggers; if false, the block never executes.

questions.question: An infinite loop `for {}` will crash Go immediately.
questions.explanation: Infinite loops are actively required to keep servers and game loops running.

questions.question: When building a web server, you use a _____ loop to listen for client connections continuously.
questions.explanation: A perpetual `for {}` loop acts as the engine.

questions.question: What character separates the index and value when iterating over a range? `for i _ val := range collection`
questions.explanation: A comma cleanly separates the dual outputs provided by the `range` keyword: `for i, val := range...`.
